chunk_summary: This section discusses the integration of vitamin A, iodine, and zinc-ORS programs into pediatric nutrition care, emphasizing prophylaxis schedules, deficiency management, and public health linkages.
learning_objectives: Understand the national vitamin A prophylaxis schedule and its integration with immunization programs.
Recognize clinical signs of vitamin A deficiency and appropriate therapeutic interventions.
Learn the importance of iodized salt usage and its role in preventing iodine deficiency disorders.
Understand the role of zinc and ORS in managing pediatric diarrhoea and its integration with nutrition care.
key_facts: Vitamin A prophylaxis includes 100,000 IU at 9 months, 200,000 IU at 16–18 months, and every 6 months up to 5 years.
Therapeutic vitamin A dosing is required for clinical signs like night blindness, Bitot spots, and corneal ulceration.
Iodized salt should be stored in a covered container away from heat and moisture to maintain iodine content.
Zinc dosing for diarrhoea is 10 mg/day for infants aged 2–6 months and 20 mg/day for children over 6 months, for 14 days.
Zinc-ORS therapy should be combined with continued feeding and hygiene counseling during diarrhoea episodes.
reference_quote: For vitamin A prophylaxis, national schedules deliver 100,000 IU at 9 months with measles immunization, then 200,000 IU at 16–18 months with booster immunizations, followed by 200,000 IU every 6 months up to 5 years.
document_key_topics: Vitamin A prophylaxis and deficiency management
Iodine deficiency prevention and household practices
Zinc-ORS therapy for diarrhoea and nutrition integration
